section2.heading: 2. Never mislead with data
section2.intro: Charts must represent data truthfully and support informed decisions, not visual emphasis.
bullets: Do not manipulate axis scales to exaggerate differences.
Start bar charts at zero unless there is a documented reason not to.
Clearly label units, timeframes and scope of the data.
Avoid 3D styling or visual effects that can distort perception.

anatomy.heading: Chart anatomy
anatomy.intro: Every chart must include the core elements required for clarity and accurate interpretation. The title can optionally include either a subtitle or a callout value.
anatomy.srNote: Numbered markers on the chart map to the anatomy list.
anatomy.canvasAriaLabel: Annotated bar chart showing chart anatomy elements including title, subtitle, callout, axis labels, data labels, legend, scale and metadata.

items.label: Title:
items.text: Use a descriptive title that states what the data represents, and include timeframe and unit where relevant.

items.label: Subtitle (optional):
items.text: Use a subtitle directly beneath the title to clarify unit, scope or measure. Use either a subtitle or a callout in most cases, not both.

items.label: Callout (optional):
items.text: Use a callout directly beneath the title to emphasise one key figure. Use either a subtitle or a callout in most cases, not both.

items.label: Axis labels:
items.text: Label each axis clearly with unit and time interval. Avoid abbreviations that are not widely understood.

items.label: Data labels:
items.text: Label data directly where practical, especially for bar and column values.

items.label: Legend:
items.text: Use a legend only when direct labels are not practical. Keep it close to the chart and readable at 200% zoom.

items.label: Scale:
items.text: Use consistent scales. Start bar charts at zero unless you have a documented reason not to, and indicate any truncation.

items.label: Source and metadata:
items.text: Include source, last updated date, and any definitions required for interpretation.

items.label: Spacing and alignment:
items.text: Keep spacing consistent between bars, labels and grid lines so the chart remains scannable.

section3.heading: 3. Make the message obvious
section3.intro: Remove clutter and make the key insight clear at a glance. A good chart answers one question well rather than several questions poorly.
bullets: Limit the number of data series.
Avoid clutter or unnecessary visual elements.
Use annotations to highlight the key insight.
dashboardBullets: Avoid horizontal scrolling for primary information.
If metadata applies to all charts, include it at the container level.

section4.heading: 4. Meet accessibility standards
section4.intro: Charts must meet WCAG 2.2 AA and AAA where possible. Users must be able to access the same information without relying on the visual chart.

subsections.heading: Contrast
bullets: Text must meet a minimum contrast ratio of 4.5:1 (WCAG 1.4.3).
Graphical elements such as bars, lines, data points and focus indicators must meet a minimum contrast ratio of 3:1 against adjacent colours and background (WCAG 1.4.11).
Test contrast in the rendered environment, not just in design files.

subsections.heading: Use of colour
subsections.intro: Colour must not be the only way to communicate meaning (WCAG 1.4.1). When differentiating data series:
bullets: Use direct labels where possible.
Use different marker shapes or stroke styles.
Avoid relying solely on red and green distinctions.
Confirm charts remain understandable in greyscale.

subsections.heading: Keyboard access
subsections.intro: Interactive charts must support full keyboard operation (WCAG 2.1.1). This includes:
bullets: All interactive elements are focusable.
Logical tab order.
Visible focus indicators.
Focus is not hidden or obscured (WCAG 2.4.11).
Interactive targets are at least 24×24px (WCAG 2.5.8).
Test keyboard interaction manually.

subsections.heading: Text alternatives
subsections.intro: Users must be able to access the same information without relying on the visual chart. Provide:
bullets: A semantic data table.
A short summary of the key insight where appropriate.
A data table that reflects the same dataset as the chart.
Static image exports without alternatives are not acceptable.

subsections.heading: Zoom and responsiveness
subsections.intro: Charts must remain usable at 200% zoom and 375px viewport width. Ensure:
bullets: Labels do not overlap.
Primary information remains visible.
Horizontal scrolling does not hide critical data.

subsections.heading: Tool limitations
bullets: Some third-party tools may restrict accessibility customisation.
If a tool cannot meet WCAG requirements, document the limitation.
Provide an accessible alternative where possible.
Accessibility responsibility remains with the product team.

section6.heading: 6. Configure tools with good defaults
section6.intro: DDS does not prescribe tools. Tool defaults are not automatically compliant.
bullets: Apply the official NSW chart theme where available.
Override default colour and typography settings.
Test accessibility in the rendered environment.
If a tool cannot meet requirements, provide an alternative.
Consider accessibility capability when selecting vendors.
